author: @ananddharwar2198
text: Best way to compost veggie scraps is to mix it with leaf litter available and blow air into the mass from bottom up. The oxygen helps thermophilic bacteria proliferate and reduce the veggie matter to fine , black earth smelling compost over 45-60 days  in my part of the world ie India. This might not work very well in colder regions  where feeding the veggie scraps to a bio methanation plant might be a better idea. One gets methane as gaseous fuel besides valuable nitrogenous sludge as fertiliser.

author: @hootsmin

author: @hootsmin
text: I was thinking about buying a large unit that can cook about 200L to, I think around 60 degrees C or so, I'm not going to make compost in it, I want to cook my soil mix and kill all the parasites, microbes and fungi, then I will re-inoculate the resultant mix.  I don't think these are entirely non useful, though those small units do not interest me.  People should certainly get a Bokashi bucket and a worm farm first if they want to succeed at composting.  I have those crappy solid black plastic composters, no air moves in these, you want to drop some air pipes in these if you have them and then they will work properly, I put a drain pipe with holes in it up the middle of the composter it has a bend on the bottom of the pipe and draws air in the hole at the bottom on the front of the unit, works very well now.

author: @barbufodor1186
text: Did once a small composting pile in the backyard, about a cubic meter at the beginning ( of course  having a relatively close to the ideal  30:1 (C :N) ratio ) and ran a dryer duct (with a small pc fan) through it to harness the heat generated by the composting/ microbes. It is not trivial to maintain the heat pile alive but it is doable and definitely can be used to climatize a small "hut" during some cold days. Similarly, yard/kitchen waste can be used in an enclosed system to produce  methane/biogas  that can be used for cooking, steam engine, etc..

replies.author: @hootsmin
replies.text: Lookup 'Jean Pain way'.  He puts a biogas digester into the center of a huge compost pile of wood chippings and stuff.

replies.author: @barbufodor1186
replies.text: @hootsmin This is a great reference.  Thank you!

author: @samuraiBSD
text: While I appreciate that these devices aren't everything they claim to be, I do have to point out that there's absolutely a market for something like these. I live in an apartment with no balcony and no usable space nearby to have an actual compost bin or pile. It would be absolutely incredible if I could still put my food waste to use somehow. I'd love compost for my indoor garden, for example. So yes, obviously don't spend dirty energy on something nature can do for free, but I think everyone should understand that apartment dwellers *also* might want the ability to compost, and these things aren't "solving a problem that doesn't exist" so much as "addressing a problem that does exist with technology that won't solve said problem."

author: @ZsOtherBrother
text:
One more thought... It's not a big stretch for me to extend Robert's recommendation to "buy [only] what you need" (08:10), to machines as well.
I'm talking about Vitamix's recommendation to "compost it first" (03:47).
Nutrients are not the only valuable thing in compost, the organisms in the compost are just as valuable, and in some respects even more valuable, than the nutrients... so taking live compost and dehydrating it at 120°c, killing all the organisms in the process, is actually DEPRECIATING the live compost and reducing its benefits.
So IMHO the proper recommendation should have been "just compost it"!

author: @Milkybar3320011
text: Get a micro pig, these will recycle your kitchen waste and either be a great pet or a future Sunday lunch 😆

author: @ZsOtherBrother
text:
I really like the comments on this channel, the videos are thought provoking and people are responding with creative ideas and useful, experience based information.
Here are some of my thoughts on the matter of waste reduction:

In nature, every byproduct from one process becomes food for other processes.

The chickens in our yard are excellent self-propagating composters, and on top of digesting food scraps and turning them into plant food, they also produce eggs for us, and keep the insect population from exploding.


I realize that not everyone can grow chickens, but there are other solutions, for example: almost anyone can keep composting worms in a small bin, (I use a 10L bucket with holes in the lid and bottom, for air and drainage, respectively).
The resulting "humus" and drained leachate will do wonders for your house plants.

For the more technologically inclined, (and adventurous:), solutions like hydrothermal carbonisation, which Robert presented in another video, are an excellent way to turn "waste" into usable resources.

I find that permaculture's three 'R's are an easy way to help keep me on track:  REDUCE  |  REUSE / REPURPOSE  |  RECYCLE.
(I know, that's FOUR 🤔, but "repurpose" is just a subset of "reuse", so...)

Last but not least, whichever way you choose, make sure you enjoy it, if it becomes "work" or a "chore" the chances of you keeping it going are slim to none.
If it's a game, your children (and the child within you:) will volunteer to help.
Why work hard to save the planet when you can play AND save the planet at the same time, right?!?

Thank you Robert for this channel and your time.

author: @Bennyboy-dog

author: @JehuMcSpooran
text: I've experimented with a few different types of composting over the years and the best I find is the good old worm farm. All our food waste (even meat) goes in a small indoor bin and is emptied into the farm once a week. By this time, there is a fair bit of decomposition going on and the bin juice down the bottom is pretty feral but this pre-digestion makes it easier for the worms to break down the food. Bokashi systems ferment the food first and reduces the smell a great deal. The worms love it. All the water from the worm farm is collected in a bucket and spread on the garden as a liquid fertiliser. I'm experimenting with bubbling air through this water to increase its nitrate content. All the worm castings are collected every now and again. It has the consistency of ice cream. I keep it in old potting mix bags and add to the garden as needed. Any bones are collected to make bone char later. I also have a tumbling bin which is great for grass clippings and an old motor oil barrel that I washed out and use for weeds.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice tips mate - cheers

author: @Alacritous
text: For composting human waste, guidelines are either compost the waste for 18 months minimum via regular means or raise the temperature of the waste above ~72°C to kill off the pathogens before it is safe to use on a garden for the production of human food (this is of course in addition to the time it takes to reduce the material via normal composting). If an electric composter could do that, then it would be useful in composting human waste.  It's not exactly efficient as such but would speed the processing of human waste.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: which guidelines are those? I was reading about composting toilets and they say anything between 2 years and an hour! - it seems to depend on temperature - I get a sense there is a lot of confusion about this particular subject.

replies.author: @Alacritous
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I did my research a long time ago, when Y2K was a threat looming on the horizon. I just had a look around and there's a LOT of new agey BS out there now.    Back then the information I read said human pathogens could live in soil for up to 18 months so I used that as a target for calculating rotation schedules for compost with human feces in it (cesspits).  That duration can be reduced substantially if you can raise the compost temperature above ~130°F(54°C) then you can shorten that time considerably. The higher the temperature, the shorter the time needed to kill the pathogens. If you can raise it above 72°C then it just takes a few hours to sterilize the material. Then you can just treat it as regular compost.

There is a certain logic to dehydrate all the compostable waste generated in a city and transport it it a place where it can be composted where the methane can be collected and then the compost used as fertilizer.
This of course will only work with a sound product that has a long lifespan and if the energy source to dehydrate the compostable material is green.

Quite literally between the human waste and waste food we produce we would be able to fertilize all the crops we need to eat. The challenge is to do so safely.

author: @DanielSMatthews
text: In cities it is better to redirect all waste organic matter to pyrolysis plants which produce electricity from steam driven turbines at the initial heat stage, secondary (lower) heat for district or industrial heating, and biogas as an industrial input or for additional heating and electricity generation. Anywhere with 1/4 acre per home or more can have chickens and compost bins. The key point being that ideal solutions are context specific.

author: @scotttovey
text:
It'd be more advantageous to purchase an incinerator toilet that turns it to ashes.
Or a composting toilet that actually begins the composting process.

It is also possible to build a composting unit out of a 5 gallon bucket. Turn the bucket on it's side, add some bearings so that you can easily turn the bucket like a drum and perhaps add add something to act as a bar.

Add holes for aeration and temperature control, then set it out on the porch.

Just keep in mind to not do this while living in an apartment. 
You may end up evicted should the stench become too strong.

Come to think of it, an old dryer drum, washer drum or cement mixer would be a good fit for this.

Coffee and manure are good nitrogen for the heap.

author: @boncambier1607
text: My grandmother impulse-bought the vitamix unit earlier this year, and my family and I were *very* skeptical, but she has actually made a very good case why it works for her. She is too frail to go pitchforking through a compost pile and heaving it onto her vegetable garden, and while it's not perfect, the dehydrator allows her to blend dried kitchen scraps into her garden soil with just a hand trowel, a little bit at a time. She lives alone, and her "food waste" is not the result of irresponsible purchase and spoilage of food, it's just scraps like eggshells, onion skins, and banana peels. The dehydrated material does not smell, a huge improvement not only inside the house, but also outside where the smell of rot attracts foxes, the neighbor's dog, etc. Producing good compost takes a lot more time and work than the cheerful organic gardener at the farmer's market would have you believe. When my grandmother was younger, she did that work and had a fantastic compost heap, but for her, those days are over. So I think hers may just be one of the acceptable use cases for one of these admittedly preposterous machines.

author: @smob0
text: I have done a fair bit of research and playing around with composters. Small composting systems tend to be roughly  55gal drum sized, and if you make them right can make compost in only 6 weeks. You need several different types of materials usually, and the populations of microorganisms have to go through various boom/bust cycles to break different components down. Bacteria tend to process sugars quickly, fungus process woody materials but it's slow. You also need an appropriate water content, so if someone gave me a 5 gallon pail of Lomi dust to compost, I'd probably have to soak it first, which makes me wonder what the point of it is.

author: @SchwaAlien
text: Can you work out a system that food waste can be made to intentionally produce methane that can be captured and put to use for heating or cooking?  I’ve seen some random videos pop up on the subject but I love the way you approach inventing things since it tends to be more general guidance than specific details which is what we need to be able to implement our own systems using what’s available to us.

replies.author: @joinedupjon
replies.text: This is what would happen in my area if you threw the food waste into an under sink garbage grinder. It'd go to the sewage works and the solids are fed into a biogas digester to make methane... the solid stuff that comes out of the digester is sold to farmers to fertilise their fields. My 'gut' feeling is it's probably more efficient to do it in a municipal size plant whenever that's possible.

author: @totherarf
text:
Call me an Old Codger (if you must), but things tend to go in cycles if you wait long enough (yes Penny Rounds WILL make a comeback)!

I remember t' fust day at t' pit ..... Sorry wrong story! ;o)
I remember when almost every household had their own waste reduction machine in their homes. It used to be inbuilt in a suitable wall and as a by-product created a lot of useful heat. We would often all gather round this mythic machine and one leader of the household would often be in charge of shoveling coal on it when needed! Of course in the newfangled age of the Gas Fire coal fires dwindled away into obsolescence!

OK the real reason I am posting this is I have had an Idea and feel feedback may be called for to refine my invention. It would seem that one of the biggest drawbacks to these niche gadgets is the cost of producing them. I have ilimin ated over 99% of this cost! Further distribution of my device will be zero ..... because everyone has one already! I mean, how good can it get?

Right, My device is a Rotary Compostles Composter! ...... Ta Da!
You may well be scratching your head on this so I shall tell all.

Everyone has a Tumble Drier. Simply shovel your waste food scraps in and bob it on an extra dry cycle! All the Steam given off by those table top devices is conveniently vented outside so there are no smells eather! Now your Mrs might be like mine .... initially unimpressed, but I came along with a foolproof method of cleaning the beast so she has not realized ..... yet! As I said bob her on an extra dry cycle and when it is finnished simply hoover up the dry remains. Et Voila!
You can even use those reuseable vac bags, so you can tip the "evidence" directly in your garden!
Genius or what?

author: @TheBaconWizard
text:
In comparison, it would be relatively simple to build an autoclave with a built-in induction heater of the type you showed us, and turn just about any organic matter into something useful. 
I almost WANT a drone-brella though, that seems like fun! Especially on a windy day XD

author: @ritualchaos7182
text: How about a wormery.... they produce very good compost and you feed one of Gods creatures!!! 😁

replies.author: @marcogallazzi9049
replies.text: Technically, it´s a different process and produce. Wormcastings are used as fertilizer in small portions, because it is very strong stuff. Compost is a bit of a mystery, as it´s both a substrate because of it´s structure and ability to retain moisture (some people do their planting directly in just compost), as it is a fertilizer.

replies.author: @ritualchaos7182
replies.text: @marcogallazzi9049  As you say... a bit of a mystery... technically!!

replies.author: @annebeignatborde1832
replies.text:
I have a wormery indoors and a compost bin outdoors. Basically same stuff goes in both except  that oignon, garlic and other strong smelling veggies and citrus go into the composter and banana skins are for the worms. Then the compost bin receives the grass clippings and a lot of tree leaves whereas it's mainly paper and cardboard.
The common rules for both of them are no meat, no dairy, and no processed food.

replies.author: @ritualchaos7182

replies.author: @annebeignatborde1832
replies.text: @ritualchaos7182 yeah I like it too but all the experts out there say that red wrigglers don't and as my worm bins are indoors... Same for citrus - which causes problems in the compost bin if there's too much (due to the oil in the skins).

author: @marcogallazzi9049
text: I´ve been into composting for some time now. For hot compost, the faster process made by bacteria, there´s a ratio of "greens" (nitrogen) to "browns" (carbon), something like 1:30. Plus it needs a certain volume (one cubic meter at least), humidity and oxigen to work properly. If it gets too hot, you kill off the microbes. If you take the water away, it will indeed produce no compost at all, like you sayd. There´s an interesting design with a fancy name, the "bioreactor" :), that is meant to reduce the work of constant turning, which is pretty tiring, spetially for people of age or with some handicap. Then of course there´s the cold compost, made basically with fungal activity, where you can just make a pile of stuff and wait longer. Both give excellent results btw.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @saiello2061
text: Check out the Japanese cultural phenomenon and artform of "Shindogu". The idea is to invent devices that at first glance appear to be useful but on closer inspection are utterly ridiculous and impractical. They're refered to as "Unuseless" devices, devices that have the APPEARANCE of usefulness ( hence the "un" ), but are ACTUALLY useless. People might remember those magazines that would get shoved through your letterbox, full of new inventions to make your life easier. These magazines were publications where "good" ideas go to die... 😁

author: @ProlificInvention
text: I love how that guy thinks, as far as composting goes the best design I've seen was built at my elementary school in the 1980s using basically a 5ft x 5ft x 5ft box made from deck wood.  It had a hinged lid on top with a hydraulic jack built in to lift it open when necessary, and the bottom had pull out drawers that collected "compost tea" and it had the ability to remove the screen on bottom to clean out the soil twice a year.  They put all the grass clippings and cafeteria food waste including the biodegradable food trays my school was testing out in there and we used the soil for our school garden.  I've seen every design of composter on the market including these modern kitchen versions and still like the schools design over all others.  Part of the secret was there was a slatted top that allowed some water intrusion which would percolate through and turn into compost tea, which was occasionally dumped back on the top to re-percolate through which would cause very fast decomposition.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am just about to build my own compost heap mate - thanks for the advice

replies.author: @ivanolsen8596
replies.text: I have a similar setup to which I introduced earthworms.

replies.author: @Skaadi89
replies.text: I used an old 55 gallon plastic drum installed a hose spigot at the bottom to drain off the compost tea straight into a garden hose with afew kitchen sink screens on the inside behind the spigot to keep the chunks out of the hose. My gardens had soaker hoses with quick connections and I would just drain the tea straight into the gardens. The system worked really well and the plants all grew wonderfully

replies.author: @ProlificInvention
replies.text: @Skaadi89 That sounds like a great system

replies.author: @Skaadi89
replies.text: It seemed to work quite well had to put some screens over the top to keep the critters out of it reused an old fireplace screen for that. I like to recycle any material I can

author: @Gainn
text:
We just lob everything into a lidded water tub with a load of tiny holes in the bottom.
Allows the water to drain away and doesn't kill off the good stuff.
The water drains off into our vegetable patch irrigation system, so everything is used.
